title: Penile Doppler Ultrasound: A Key to Male Health

seo.title: Penile Doppler Ultrasound: Technique, Interpretation & Uses
seo.description: Learn about penile Doppler ultrasound technique and interpretation. This test evaluates blood flow to diagnose erectile dysfunction and other penile conditions.

markdown:
"

Doppler ultrasound is a procedure that uses the Doppler effect to produce moving images of the body fluids, the tissues, and their relative speeds to the transmitter. Let’s review the role of Penile Doppler Ultrasound.

## **What is Penile Doppler Ultrasound?**

This is a technique used to predict the response of the ED (Erectile Dysfunction) to vasodilation medications that enhance blood flow to a person’s penis. Moreover, it helps the urologist in determining and documenting if you have an outflow or inflow type of erectile dysfunction. There are several causes of erectile dysfunction. Some diseases like arteriosclerosis also referred to as plaque in the blood vessels, [diabetes](/bangalore/lab-test/diabetes-sugar-test-dst), and high blood pressure exert their damaging effects on the blood vessels all over the body, including the penis. During the process, the doctor ultrasounds the entire penis.

## **What diseases can be detected?**

Penile ultrasound plays a crucial role in detecting and treating diseases concerning the penis. By the Doppler ultrasound of the penis, some pathologies that can be detected have been discussed below:

1. **Evaluation of erectile dysfunction:** Penile Doppler ultrasound helps assess the blood flow to the penis during an erection. It can determine if there are any vascular issues contributing to ED. By using Doppler technology, which measures the speed and direction of blood flow, it can identify any abnormalities in the penile arteries or veins that may be causing or contributing to erectile problems.

2. **Diagnosis of Peyronie’s disease:** [Penile Doppler ultrasound](/bangalore/ultrasound-scan/penile-doppler-ultrasound-scan) can aid in the diagnosis of Peyronie’s disease by visualizing the plaques or scar tissue within the penis. It can provide information about the plaques’ location, size, and extent, which can help guide treatment decisions.

3. **Assessment of priapism:** Priapism is a persistent, painful erection that can occur without sexual stimulation. Penile Doppler ultrasound can help differentiate between the two types of priapism: ischemic (low-flow) and non-ischemic (high-flow). It can identify any abnormal shunting or increased blood flow in the penis, which can guide appropriate management strategies.

4) **Penile cancer**

With an ultrasound of the penis, penile cancer can be detected. The results also help doctors determine the extent of cancer so that they can determine the treatment plan.

**Procedure**

This test will be performed when you are lying on your back on an examining room table. Using a very small needle, a penile injection of a vasodilator agent named papaverine will be performed. This helps to relax the erectile muscle in the penis and dilate the penile arteries. This results in an increase in blood flow to the penis and give the individual an erection. You will experience only a bit of insect bite sensation with this injection. The blood flow will be measured in the penile artery on both sides of the individual’s penis using a small ultrasound probe positioned along the shaft of the penis. During this portion of this procedure, there is no pain involved.

When the male genitals show abnormal symptoms, for a timely examination and treatment, you must visit a reputable medical facility.

Find the [Diagnostic Centre in Bangalore](/) for the best service.

title: Cadabam's Guide to Managing Diabetes with the Right Foods

seo.title: Best Foods to Manage Diabetes & Lower Blood Sugar | Guide
seo.description: Discover the top foods to include in your diet to manage diabetes and lower blood sugar naturally. Expert dietary advice for better glucose control.

markdown:
Maintaining healthy blood sugar levels is vital for individuals with [diabetes](/bangalore/lab-test/diabetes-sugar-test-dst). Besides proper medical care and lifestyle modifications, a well-balanced diet has a vital role in managing diabetes effectively. Here are the top 10 foods that can help control diabetes and [lower blood sugar levels](/blogs/understanding-hypoglycemia-low-blood-sugar):

## **Non-Starchy Vegetables**

Non-starchy vegetables are an excellent choice for individuals with diabetes as they offer exceptional health benefits. These vegetables are rich in essential minerals and vitamins that help regulate [blood sugar](/blogs/understanding-random-blood-sugar-rbs-level-ranges) levels. Since they are whole foods with minimal sugar content and high fiber levels, they can be enjoyed without worrying about spikes in blood sugar. Fresh vegetables are better than canned or frozen vegetables loaded with added salt or sauce. Examples of non-starchy vegetables include artichokes, broccoli, asparagus, cabbage, green beans, cauliflower, celery, tomatoes, cucumbers, hearts of palm, mushrooms, olives, onions, avocados, squashes, zucchini, and more.

## **Fatty Fish**

Fatty fish provide significant amounts of omega-3 fatty acids like DHA and EPA, which protect the heart from potential diabetes-related complications. These fatty acids support blood vessel health, reduce inflammation and enhance arterial function. Considering that individuals with diabetes have a higher risk of heart disease and stroke, incorporating fatty fish into a balanced diet can help reduce the chances of serious complications while providing satiating protein.

## **Leafy Greens**

Leafy greens have lesser digestible carbs and more nutrients. This means that consuming leafy greens such as spinach and kale will have minimal impact on blood sugar levels regardless of the quantity consumed. Additionally, these greens are high in vitamin C, which is immensely beneficial in managing diabetes, promoting well-being, and protecting against diabetic complications that affect the eyes.

## **Nuts and Eggs**

Nuts and eggs are other healthy options that aid in [blood sugar management](/blogs/understanding-normal-blood-sugar-levels) for individuals with diabetes. Nuts are rich in fiber and low in digestible carbs, which makes them suitable for maintaining stable blood sugar levels. However, it is important to select certain types of nuts like almonds, Brazil nuts, cashews, hazelnuts, macadamia nuts, pecans, pistachios, and walnuts.

Eggs are known to improve insulin sensitivity, reduce inflammation, and offer antioxidant benefits that protect against diseases. Even the yolk is recommended as it contains most of the nutrients.

## **Cinnamon and Turmeric**

Spices like cinnamon and turmeric are incredibly wonderful in controlling diabetes. Cinnamon controls blood sugar levels, improves insulin sensitivity, and reduces hemoglobin A1c levels. Turmeric reduces inflammation and blood sugar levels, lowers the risk of heart disease, and benefits kidney health. Mixing turmeric with a pinch of black pepper enhances the absorption of curcumin (an essential compound present in turmeric) in the body by up to 2,000%.

## **Seeds**

Seeds like flax seeds and chia seeds have been found to be beneficial in diabetes management. Chia seeds are rich in fiber, low in digestible carbs, and can lower blood sugar levels. Flaxseeds are known to improve blood sugar control, lessen the risk of stroke and heart disease. Flaxseeds should be roasted and/or ground before consuming. Whole flaxseeds are difficult to absorb.

## **Apple Cider Vinegar**

The fermented acetic acid in apple cider vinegar helps improve insulin sensitivity. It also reduces fasting blood sugar levels and blood sugar response when consumed with meals that are rich in carbs. Diluting apple cider vinegar with water is advisable to avoid damaging teeth and the esophagus.

## **Strawberries**

Strawberries are a sweet option that offers antioxidant benefits and has been shown to reduce cholesterol and insulin levels after a meal. If you are not fond of strawberries, then you can have other types of berries like raspberries, blueberries, or blackberries. Like strawberries, they also have low sugar content compared to fruits like apples and bananas.

## **Dairy Rich in Probiotics**

Dairy foods rich in probiotics are immensely beneficial for health. Greek yoghurt is high in probiotics and low in sugar. It improves blood sugar control and lessen the risk of heart disease. It is best to select unflavored Greek yoghurt to avoid added sugars and excessive processing, which can contribute to [blood sugar spikes](/blogs/understanding-hyperglycemia-high-blood-sugar).

## **Natural Fats**

Incorporating natural fats into the diet helps control diabetes. Extra-virgin olive oil is known for its huge health benefits. It helps lessen the risk of heart disease and has antioxidant properties that reduce inflammation, protect cells, and lowers blood pressure. Other beneficial natural fats include various nut oils, coconut oil, chicken fat, avocado oil, tallow, duck fat, lard, coconut milk, and unsweetened coconut cream.

## **How to Get Tested for Diabetes**

If you feel you have signs and symptoms of diabetes, you can visit [Cadabams Diagnostics](/). We are a trusted diagnostic centre in Bangalore that offers diabetes testing services including diabetic packages, along with several other tests, for you. Call us to learn more about our services.

title: Hyperglycemia Symptoms, Risks, and Control Strategies

seo.title: Hyperglycemia: Understanding High Blood Sugar & Management
seo.description: Learn about hyperglycemia, its causes, symptoms, and complications. Understand how to manage elevated glucose levels and prevent diabetes risks.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of high blood sugar?
faqs.answer: High blood sugar, also known as hyperglycemia, can be caused by various factors, often related to how your body makes and uses insulin and glucose. Common causes include: Diabetes Type 1 and Type 2, Poor Diet, Stress,Hormonal Changes.

faqs.question: How can I recognize the symptoms of high blood sugar, especially in women?
faqs.answer: Recognizing the symptoms of high blood sugar (hyperglycemia) is essential for managing and treating it effectively since symptoms can vary slightly between men and women. Here are common signs and symptoms of Blurred Vision, Fatigue, Weight Loss, and Mood Changes.

faqs.question: What do the different levels on a blood sugar chart indicate?
faqs.answer: A blood sugar chart helps monitor and interpret glucose (sugar) levels in the blood, providing essential information for diabetes management. The different levels generally indicate Normal Blood Sugar Levels, Prediabetes, and Diabetes.

faqs.question: What are the potential short-term and long-term complications of high blood sugar?
faqs.answer:
High blood sugar, or hyperglycemia, can lead to a variety of complications if not properly managed. These complications can be categorised into short-term and long-term issues:

- **Short-term Complications:**Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS), Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar).
- **Long-term Complications:**Cardiovascular Disease, Nerve Damage (Neuropathy), Gastrointestinal Issues.

faqs.question: How can high blood sugar be effectively managed or prevented?
faqs.answer:
Effectively managing or preventing high blood sugar involves several strategies, often focusing on lifestyle adjustments and medical management. Here are key approaches:

1. Healthy Eating
2. Regular Exercise
3. Monitor Blood Sugar Levels
4. Manage Stress

markdown:
High blood sugar, or hyperglycemia, refers to an excessive amount of glucose circulating in the bloodstream. It is most commonly linked to diabetes, a metabolic disorder where the body either doesn’t produce enough insulin or can’t effectively use the insulin it produces.

High blood sugar can result from various factors, including dietary choices, physical inactivity, medication side effects, or underlying health conditions.

## What Causes High Blood Sugar?

High blood sugar, or hyperglycemia, can be caused by several factors, primarily related to the body’s ability to produce or use insulin effectively. Here are the main causes:

**Diabetes:** The most common cause of high blood sugar in diabetes, particularly:

1. **Type 1 Diabetes:** Where the body does not produce insulin, leading to uncontrolled blood sugar levels.
2. **Type 2 Diabetes:** Where the body does not use insulin effectively, often related to insulin resistance.

**Dietary Choices:**Consuming foods high in sugar, carbohydrates, or calories can cause a spike in blood sugar levels, primarily if the body cannot process or utilise glucose effectively.

**Physical Inactivity:**Lack of exercise can contribute to weight gain and insulin resistance, increasing blood sugar levels.

**Medication Effects:**Certain medications, such as steroids or beta-blockers, can increase blood sugar levels as a side effect.

### Insulin Resistance and Pancreatic Issues

Insulin resistance and pancreatic issues are significant factors that can contribute to the development of high blood sugar and diabetes. Here’s how they are related:

**Causes of Insulin Resistance:**

- **Genetics:** Some individuals have a genetic predisposition to insulin resistance.
- **Obesity:** Excess fatty tissue, especially around the abdomen, can increase the body’s resistance to insulin.
- **Sedentary Lifestyle:** Lack of physical activity can contribute to the development of insulin resistance.
- **Poor Diet:** Diets high in refined carbs, sugars, and unhealthy fats can lead to insulin resistance.
- **Age:**The risk increases with age, particularly over 45 years.
- **Hormonal Imbalances:**Conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) can contribute to insulin resistance.

**Causes of Pancreatic issues:**

- **Pancreatitis:**Inflammation of the pancreas can impair its ability to function properly, affecting insulin production.
- **Pancreatic Cancer:** Tumours in the pancreas can interfere with its normal functions, including insulin production.
- **Pancreatectomy:**Removing all or part of the pancreas can decrease or eliminate insulin production.
- **Cystic Fibrosis:**This condition can damage the pancreas, affecting its ability to produce insulin.

### Risk Factors for High Blood Sugar

High blood sugar, or hyperglycemia, can be caused by various factors and conditions, impacting people with or without diabetes. Here are some common risk factors for high blood sugar:

1. **Diabetes:** The most direct association with high blood sugar is diabetes, including both type 1 and type 2 diabetes. In type 1 diabetes, the body does not produce insulin, whereas in type 2, the body either doesn’t produce enough insulin or can’t use it effectively.
2. **Diet:** Consuming large quantities of carbohydrates, especially refined carbs and sugary foods, can cause blood sugar levels to spike.
3. **Physical Inactivity:**Lack of regular exercise can increase insulin resistance, leading to higher blood sugar levels.
4. **Stress:** Physical and emotional stress can prompt the body to release hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, which can increase blood sugar.

## Symptoms of High Blood Sugar

High blood sugar, or hyperglycemia, manifests through a variety of symptoms. Some people may not notice the signs until blood sugar levels are significantly high. Recognizing these symptoms is crucial, especially for individuals with diabetes. Here are common symptoms of high blood sugar:

- **Increased Thirst and Urination:** High blood sugar levels cause the kidneys to work harder to filter and absorb excess sugar, leading to frequent urination and dehydration, which causes increased thirst.
- **Fatigue:** Elevated blood sugar can impact your energy levels, making you fatigued.
- **Blurred Vision:** High blood sugar can cause the eye’s lens to swell, changing your ability to see and resulting in blurry vision.
- **Headaches:** When blood sugar levels are high, headaches can frequently occur as a symptom.
- **Dry Mouth and Skin:** Dehydration from frequent urination can lead to dry mouth and skin.

## High Blood Sugar Levels and Charts

[Understanding blood sugar](/blogs/understanding-random-blood-sugar-rbs-level-ranges) levels through charts is crucial for those managing diabetes or monitoring their risk. Here’s a comprehensive overview of what you need to know about high blood sugar levels and how to use charts effectively.

### Understanding Blood Sugar Levels Chart

Depending on the country, blood sugar levels are typically measured in milligrams per deciliter (mg/dL) or millimoles per litre (mmol/L). Here’s a quick rundown on what different levels generally mean:

**Normal:**

- Fasting (no caloric intake for at least 8 hours): Below 100 mg/dL (5.6 mmol/L)
- 2 hours after eating: Below 140 mg/dL (7.8 mmol/L)

**Prediabetes:**

- Fasting: 100-125 mg/dL (5.6-6.9 mmol/L)
- 2 hours after eating: 140-199 mg/dL (7.8-11.0 mmol/L)

**Diabetes:**

- Fasting: 126 mg/dL (7 mmol/L) or higher on two separate tests
- 2 hours after eating: 200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) or higher

### How to Read a Blood Sugar Level Chart?

When looking at a [blood sugar level chart](/blogs/understanding-normal-blood-sugar-levels), it’s important to remember these key factors:

- **Identify the Measurement Units:** First, check whether the chart uses mg/dL or mmol/L. This will help you understand the values correctly.
- **Look at the Time Frames:**Blood sugar charts might show different values based on various conditions — fasting, post-meal (typically 2 hours after a meal), and sometimes even post-prandial (immediately after eating) or bedtime levels.
- **Compare Your Numbers:** Once you measure your blood sugar using a glucose metre, compare your numbers to the values listed on the chart to see which category you fall into.
- **Check for Colour Codes:**Many charts use colour coding to make it easier to interpret the results:
- **Green usually indicates normal levels.**Yellow may indicate prediabetes.
- Red often signifies diabetes or dangerously high levels.
- **Follow-Up with Guidelines:**Charts may include guidelines or suggestions for what to do if your levels fall into a particular range, such as consulting a healthcare provider if you are in the diabetes range.
- **Regular Monitoring:**Compare your results regularly with the chart to track your blood sugar control over time and understand how different foods, activities, and medications affect your levels.

## Complications and Side Effects of High Blood Sugar

High blood sugar, if not adequately controlled, can lead to various complications and side effects. These can be divided into short-term and long-term issues, each with potentially serious health impacts.

### Short-term Complications

- **Hyperglycemia:**
- High blood sugar levels can cause symptoms like increased thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, nausea, and blurred vision.
- **Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA):**
- This serious condition occurs primarily in people with type 1 diabetes, but it can also affect those with type 2 diabetes under certain conditions. DKA develops when your body starts breaking down fats at a too-fast rate. The liver processes the fat into a fuel called ketones, which causes the blood to become acidic.
- **Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Nonketotic Syndrome (HHNS):**
- This is a severe condition that can occur in individuals with type 2 diabetes, where blood sugar levels rise very high, and water is pulled from the body tissues, including the lenses of the eyes, which can lead to seizures, coma, and even death if untreated.
- **Hypoglycemia:**
- Ironically, over-treatment of high blood sugar can lead to too [low blood sugar levels](/blogs/understanding-hypoglycemia-low-blood-sugar). Symptoms include shaking, confusion, dizziness, and in severe cases, loss of consciousness.

### Long-term Complications

- **Cardiovascular Disease:**
- High blood sugar levels over time can lead to an increased risk of heart disease, including coronary artery disease with chest pain (angina), heart attack, stroke, and narrowing of arteries (atherosclerosis).
- **Nerve Damage (Neuropathy):**
- Excess sugar can injure the walls of the tiny blood vessels (capillaries) that nourish your nerves, especially in the legs. This can cause tingling, numbness, burning, or pain that usually begins at the tips of the toes or fingers and gradually spreads upward.
- **Kidney Damage (Nephropathy):**
- The kidneys contain millions of tiny blood vessel clusters that filter waste from your blood. Diabetes can damage this delicate filtering system, leading to kidney failure or irreversible end-stage kidney disease, which may require dialysis or a kidney transplant.
- **Eye Damage:**
- Diabetes can damage the retina’s blood vessels (diabetic retinopathy), potentially leading to blindness. Diabetes also increases the risk of other severe vision conditions, such as cataracts and glaucoma.
- **Foot Damage:**
- Nerve damage in the feet or poor blood flow to the feet increases the risk of various foot complications. Left untreated, cuts and blisters can develop serious infections that heal poorly. These infections may ultimately require toe, foot, or leg amputation.

## Managing High Blood Sugar

Managing high blood sugar effectively involves a multifaceted approach that combines lifestyle changes, [dietary adjustments](/blogs/top-foods-to-include-in-your-diet-to-manage-diabetes-and-lower-blood-sugar), medical treatments and regular monitoring. Here’s a breakdown of each component to help maintain optimal blood glucose levels:

1. Lifestyle Changes and Diet

- **Regular Physical Activity:**
- Exercise helps increase insulin sensitivity, allowing cells to utilise available sugar in the bloodstream better. Aim for aerobic workouts (walking, swimming, or cycling) and resistance training.
- **Healthy Eating:**

1. Adopt a balanced diet that limits sugars and simple carbohydrates. Focus on high-fibre foods (whole grains, vegetables, fruits), lean proteins, and healthy fats.
2. Plan meals and snacks to evenly distribute calorie intake throughout the day, which can help prevent blood sugar spikes.

- **Weight Management:**

1. Maintaining a healthy weight is crucial, as excess body fat can contribute to insulin resistance.
2. A modest 5-10% weight loss can significantly improve blood sugar management.

### 2. Medical Treatments and Monitoring

- **Medication Compliance:**

1. Taking those prescribed medications as directed is crucial. This may include insulin or other glucose-lowering medications.
2. Regularly consult with healthcare providers to adjust medication based on blood sugar readings.

- **Regular Blood Sugar Monitoring:**

1. Frequent monitoring can help track the effectiveness of the current management plan and avoid complications.
2. Continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) or regular use of blood glucose metres can provide insight into daily patterns and fluctuations.

- **Routine Check-Ups:**

1. Regular visits to a healthcare provider are essential for ongoing assessment and management of blood sugar levels.
2. These check-ups can also help screen for and manage potential complications, such as neuropathy, nephropathy, and retinopathy.

- **Advanced Treatment Options:**
- In some cases, healthcare providers might recommend more advanced treatment options like insulin pumps or injectable therapies that can provide more stable control over blood sugar levels.

## Prevention: Keeping Your Blood Sugar in Check

Keeping your blood sugar levels in check is crucial, especially for those at risk of diabetes or already managing the condition. Here are some effective strategies for maintaining healthy blood sugar levels:

- Balanced Diet:

Focus on a fibre-rich diet, such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and legumes.

Include lean proteins and healthy fats to help moderate blood sugar spikes.

Limit intake of refined sugars and carbohydrates, which can cause rapid increases in blood sugar.

- Regular Exercise:

Regular physical activity helps increase insulin sensitivity and allows your muscles to use sugar more effectively.

Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic or 75 minutes of vigorous activity each week, along with muscle-strengthening exercises on two or more days.

- Monitor Blood Sugar Levels:

Regular monitoring can help you understand how your diet, lifestyle, and medications affect your blood sugar. It also helps you adjust your eating habits, medications, or activities to better manage your levels.

- Maintain a Healthy Weight:

Achieving and maintaining a healthy weight helps control blood sugar levels and reduces the risk of developing type 2 diabetes. Weight management can be facilitated by diet, exercise, and lifestyle changes.

- Stay Hydrated:

Drinking enough water helps keep your blood sugar levels within healthy limits and helps the kidneys flush out excess sugar through urine.

## Elevating High Blood Sugar Excellence at Cadabam’s

Cadabam’s Diagnostics is recognized as a premier diagnostic centre in Bangalore, specialising in a range of diagnostic services for various medical conditions. Located in Banashankari, we offer comprehensive laboratory tests, radiology, and imaging services. Our state-of-the-art technology and skilled team ensure precise and dependable results.

[Cadabam’s Diagnostics](/) is committed to providing extensive diagnostic solutions to support early detection and effective management of health issues, focusing on the quality and accuracy essential for patient well-being.

Additionally, with an emphasis on physiotherapy and related treatment options, Cadabam’s Diagnostics stands out among diagnostic centres in Bangalore and nearby regions. Our facility has advanced machinery, enabling us to deliver superior diagnostic services for all health conditions. We assist in identifying your health concerns facilitating further management and care.

For more information on managing high blood sugar or to schedule a diagnostic appointment, visit our website or [contact us](\) at [info@cadabamsdiagnostics.com](mailto:info@cadabamsdiagnostics.com). Let us be your trusted partner in achieving and [maintaining optimal health](\).

title: CBC Report Explained: Unlock Your Blood Test Insights

seo.title: Complete Blood Count (CBC) Report: Understanding Your Result
seo.description: Learn to decipher your Complete Blood Count (CBC) report. Understand how RBC, WBC, platelet, and hemoglobin levels reveal your overall health status.

markdown:
When you are seeking your health report as part of your health check-up, one of the most important elements that you want to focus on is the CBC or Complete Blood Count. This blood test provides you a complete insight into the overall health and other parameters of your condition. The diagnosis can be helpful in achieving a better standard when it comes to monitoring your health.

## What is a CBC Report?

A CBC report is a blood test that can be used to measure the different parameters of your blood. The test includes the measurement of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. Each of these components can provide a complete information on the entire info on your health. This will help you identify potential issues well ahead of time before they can become serious.

The test is a good source of information about every detail of your health. It would include many conditions like infections, anemia, and leukemia among others.In fact, the Complete Blood Checkup is a part of the regular blood check. There is nothing you need to worry about it. Taking a CBC test is the best way to be proactive in your journey towards better health.

## What health conditions can CBC detect?

CBC can help detect several health conditions, which include the following –

- **Anaemia –**Low RBC content and hemoglobin and can indicate anemia, which is caused by multiple factors such as nutritional deficiencies or chronic diseases.
- **Infections –** Elevated WBC count can indicate neutrophil count. It may also suggest an ongoing infection.
- **Bleeding disorders –** An abnormal platelet count can indicate bleeding and clotting disorders. Your doctor can take up further investigation in case they find any issues.

The complete blood count can prove to be quite handy in providing you with a valuable tool for assessing your overall health. By properly understanding the components and help you can interpret results in the context of reference ranges. A regular check-up of CBC can be useful and practical in helping you avoid several diseases in staying proactive about your health and well-being.

## How is CBC Done?

For a complete blood count test, you need a lab technician who takes a sample of your blood through a needle. The blood can be taken from the vein in your arm or at the end of the bend in the elbow.

Your blood sample is then sent to laboratory for investigation. You need not take any sort of rest after the test. You can return to your regular activities. Unless the test is undertaken for any specific illness, you do not need to take any rest. title: Scrotal Doppler Ultrasound: Diagnosis and Benefits

seo.title: Scrotal Doppler Ultrasound How It Works & Applications Guide
seo.description: Learn how a scrotal Doppler ultrasound works. This test evaluates testicular blood flow to diagnose pain, swelling, torsion, and varicoceles accurately.

markdown:
Scrotal Doppler ultrasound, also known as scrotal color Doppler ultrasound, is a diagnostic imaging technique used to evaluate the blood flow within the scrotum and testicles. It is commonly employed in the assessment of various scrotal conditions and can provide valuable information about blood flow abnormalities, such as testicular torsion, varicocele, and epididymitis.

## **Here’s how scrotal Doppler ultrasound works and its applications:**

**1. Testicular torsion:** Scrotal Doppler ultrasound is frequently used in the evaluation of acute scrotal pain to assess for testicular torsion, which is a urological emergency. It can determine if there is reduced or absent blood flow to the affected testicle, helping to confirm the diagnosis and guide prompt surgical intervention.

**2. Varicocele:** Varicocele is a condition characterized by the enlargement of the veins within the scrotum. [Scrotal Doppler ultrasound](/bangalore/ultrasound-scan) can identify and assess the extent of varicoceles, providing information about the blood flow within the affected veins. This helps in the diagnosis and classification of varicoceles and aids in determining the need for treatment.

**3. Epididymitis:** Epididymitis is the inflammation of the epididymis, a coiled tube located behind the testicle. Scrotal Doppler ultrasound can help identify increased blood flow within the epididymis, which is often seen in cases of epididymitis. This finding can support the diagnosis and assist in differentiating epididymitis from other scrotal conditions.

During a scrotal Doppler ultrasound, a gel is applied to the scrotum, and a handheld transducer is moved over the area to capture images and record the Doppler signals. The procedure is generally painless and non-invasive. The technician or radiologist will evaluate the blood flow patterns, velocities, and any abnormalities within the scrotum and testicles.

It’s important to note that scrotal Doppler ultrasound is typically performed by a specialist radiologist with expertise in scrotal imaging.

If you have scrotal pain, swelling, or concerns about any scrotal-related issues, it is recommended to consult with a healthcare professional who can evaluate your symptoms, perform appropriate diagnostic tests, and provide appropriate guidance and treatment options based on your individual circumstances.

title: Understanding Liver Disease: Risk Factors and Testing Guide

seo.title: Liver Disease Risk Factors: Who Needs a Liver Test | Guide
seo.description: Understand the risk factors that mean you need a test for liver disease. Learn about symptoms, causes, and when to get your liver function checked.

markdown:
Liver disease can develop for various reasons, and certain risk factors increase the possibility of liver damage or illness. If you have one or more of these risk factors, it is imperative to consider getting tested for liver disease to perceive and address any potential problems early. Here are some common risk factors that mean you need a test for liver disease:

## **Alcohol Consumption**

Consuming alcohol beyond the permitted limit for a long period may damage your liver. It can lead to alcoholic liver disease. If you consume alcohol in large quantities regularly, you may be at risk.

## **Viral Hepatitis**

Chronic viral infections, chiefly hepatitis B and C, can lead to liver damage and cirrhosis if left untreated. If you have a history of exposure to these viruses or are in a high-risk group, such as a healthcare worker or injecting drugs, you may need a test for liver disease.

## **Family History**

If any of your family have liver disease, particularly hereditary conditions, such as Wilson’s disease or hemochromatosis, it can increase your risk. If close relatives have had liver disorders, you may be hereditarily predisposed.

## **Obesity**

Being obese or overweight can lead to NAFLD or non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. It is a condition that accumulates excess fat in your liver. NAFLD can progress to more severe forms of liver disease.

## **Diabetes**

If you are a diabetic, you may regularly need a test for liver disease. This is because people with diabetes are at a higher risk of developing non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and other liver complications. Thus, you need to control your blood sugar levels to minimize this risk.

## **High Blood Pressure**

Hypertension can pave the way to liver damage. Maintaining healthy blood pressure levels is important to lessen this risk.

## **Insecure Sex or Many Sexual Partners**

Insecure sexual practices can surge the risk of hepatitis B and C diseases, which can lead to liver disease.

## **Use of Intravenous Drugs**

Sharing needles or being involved in intravenous drug use can lead to hepatitis C or other bloodborne diseases that affect the liver.

## **Medications and Toxins**

Certain medicines, particularly when taken in high doses or for a prolonged period, can be injurious to the liver. Exposure to industrial chemicals and toxins can also pose a risk.

## **Autoimmune Disorders**

Some autoimmune diseases, such as autoimmune hepatitis, can directly affect the liver. If you have an autoimmune disorder, your liver may be at risk.

## **Mysterious Symptoms**

If you experience unexplained exhaustion, yellowing of the eyes or skin, abdominal pain, or tenacious nausea and vomiting, it may be a symptom of liver problems. You may need to address them promptly.

## **Regular Liver Function Tests**

If you have a known condition or risk factor that affects the liver, your healthcare provider may recommend regular liver function tests by testing the blood to monitor your liver health.

## **Bottom Line**

It is essential to discuss with a healthcare provider if you have one or more of these risk factors. They can evaluate the situation, recommend suitable tests, and offer guidance on lifestyle changes or treatments to guard your liver health. Early discovery and interference are crucial in managing liver disease efficiently.

title: A Guide to Breast Cancer Biopsy and Its Results

seo.title: Breast Cancer Biopsy: Process, Results, and Recovery Guide
seo.description: Understand the breast cancer biopsy process, types of biopsies, result interpretation, and recovery. A complete guide to this crucial diagnostic procedure.

markdown:
A breast cancer biopsy is a crucial diagnostic procedure used to confirm the presence of breast cancer and determine its characteristics. This minimally invasive technique allows medical professionals to extract tissue samples from the breast for detailed examination. By analyzing these samples, doctors can accurately diagnose breast cancer, determine its type, grade, and stage and suggest an appropriate treatment plan tailored to each patient’s specific needs.

There are three primary types of breast biopsies:

- Fine-needle aspiration
- Core-needle biopsy
- Surgical biopsy

## **Fine-needle aspiration**

This medical procedure is commonly chosen when there is a suspicion of a fluid-filled lump. If the lump is easily reachable or suspected to be a cystic lump filled with fluid, the doctors may opt for a fine-needle aspiration (FNA). During this procedure, under ultrasound guidance, the lump is punctured with a fine needle to draw out and discard the fluid. This causes the lump to collapse. In some cases, ultrasound guides the needle accurately by creating an internal image of the breast through sound waves.

For testing, a radiologist (a doctor specializing in medical imaging such as x-rays, ultrasounds and mammograms) will perform a similar procedure called fine needle aspiration biopsy (FNABx). This involves using a needle to extract cells from the lump for further examination.

## **Core-needle biopsy**

This procedure involves using a larger “core” or hollow needle to remove a small amount of suspicious tissue from the breast. It is performed under local anesthesia. During the procedure, a small marker is inserted to mark the biopsy location, which also aids in future surgical or imaging procedures. The core-needle biopsy is guided by specialized imaging equipment such as ultrasound or x-ray. Ultrasound-guided biopsies involve radiologists using [ultrasound](/bangalore/ultrasound-scan) to direct the needle, exactly into the tissues and taking samples for examination. A core-needle biopsy is associated with fewer side effects compared to a surgical biopsy.

## **Surgical biopsy**

A surgical biopsy is also known as wide local excision, wide local surgical biopsy, open biopsy, or lumpectomy. It is performed under local anesthesia. It is conducted in a hospital setting as the patient generally receives intravenous (IV) medications to induce drowsiness. The surgeon makes an incision on the breast and removes all or part of the abnormal lump, along with a small amount of normal-looking tissue called the margin. If the lump is visible on a mammogram or ultrasound but not easily palpable, a radiologist may place a thin wire to mark the suspicious area before the surgeon performs the biopsy. A marker is usually placed internally at the biopsy site after the procedure.

The tissue or fluid samples are examined under a microscope to detect any abnormal or cancerous cells. If no cancer cells are identified, the report will indicate that the lump’s cells are benign and non-cancerous. But if cancer cells are identified, the report will offer additional information to determine the next steps.

## **Where to Get Reliable Breast Cancer Biopsy Done?**

[Cadabams Diagnostics](/) is a reputable healthcare organization where we focus on quality and accuracy of our reports backed by advanced technology. We offer an all-inclusive range of diagnostic tests, including [breast cancer biopsy](/bangalore/ultrasound-scan/breast-biopsy-scan). Our team of qualified professionals provides patient-centric care, ensuring reliable results and a comfortable experience for individuals seeking their services.

title: Low Blood Sugar (Hypoglycemia): Causes, Symptoms, and Tips

seo.title: Hypoglycemia: Understanding Low Blood Sugar Causes & Symptom
seo.description: Learn about hypoglycemia  its causes, symptoms, and management. Understand how to recognize and treat low glucose levels to prevent complications.

faqs.question: What is hypoglycemia, and what causes low blood sugar levels?
faqs.answer: Hypoglycemia, or low blood sugar, occurs when the glucose levels in the blood drop below the normal range. The most common cause of hypoglycemia is the treatment of diabetes. People with diabetes who use insulin or certain types of diabetes medications (such as sulfonylureas) are at risk of hypoglycemia. If the balance of insulin, diet, and physical activity is not managed correctly, it can lead to low blood sugar.

faqs.question: What are the common and severe symptoms of hypoglycemia?
faqs.answer:
Hypoglycemia, or low blood sugar, can manifest through a range of symptoms varying from mild to severe. Some of the symptoms are:

1. Shakiness or Trembling
2. Sweating
3. Blurred Vision
4. Nervousness or Anxiety

faqs.question: How is hypoglycemia diagnosed, and what are the roles of blood sugar testing and continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)?
faqs.answer:
Diagnosing hypoglycemia typically involves evaluating symptoms and confirming low blood sugar levels through testing. Here’s how hypoglycemia is usually diagnosed:

1. Evaluation of Symptoms
2. Blood Sugar Testing
3. Whipple’s Triad
4. Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)

The role of CGM has become increasingly prominent, especially for those with type 1 diabetes or type 2 diabetes who have a history of hypoglycemia or hypoglycemia unawareness (a condition where the typical symptoms of low blood sugar are not recognized).

faqs.question: What are the immediate treatments and long-term management strategies for managing low blood sugar?
faqs.answer:
Managing low blood sugar, or hypoglycemia, involves immediate treatments to quickly restore blood glucose levels to standard and long-term strategies to prevent future episodes. Here’s how these approaches are typically structured:

1. Consume Fast-Acting Carbohydrates
2. Follow-Up with a Snack or Meal
3. Exercise Management

markdown:
Low blood sugar, or hypoglycemia, is characterised by an abnormally low blood glucose level, typically less than 70 mg/dL. This condition is particularly common among people with diabetes who take insulin or certain other medications.

Still, it can also occur in individuals without diabetes due to various reasons, including excessive alcohol consumption, certain medical conditions, or prolonged fasting.

## What is Hypoglycemia?

This condition is most commonly associated with diabetes, especially in individuals who take insulin or certain oral medications that increase insulin production. However, non-diabetics can also experience hypoglycemia.

This is typically due to reasons such as excessive alcohol consumption, certain medications, prolonged periods without eating, or more rare causes like hormone deficiencies or pancreatic tumours.

### Definition and Overview

Hypoglycemia refers to a medical condition where [blood sugar (glucose) levels](/bangalore/lab-test/diabetes-sugar-test-dst) drop below average. It’s generally considered hypoglycemic when blood glucose levels fall below 70 mg/dL.

Glucose is the primary energy source for the body’s cells, and a consistent supply is crucial for normal function.

### Causes of Hypoglycemia

Hypoglycemia, or low blood sugar, can be caused by various factors, primarily involving the balance (or imbalance) between insulin, [food intake](/blogs/top-foods-to-include-in-your-diet-to-manage-diabetes-and-lower-blood-sugar), and energy expenditure. Here are some common causes:

1. **Excessive Insulin or Diabetes Medication:** For people with diabetes, taking too much insulin or other diabetes medications can cause blood sugar levels to drop too low, mainly if meals are skipped or delayed.
2. **Inadequate Food Intake:**Skipping meals, eating less than usual, or eating delayed meals can significantly cause hypoglycemia if insulin or other medications are not adjusted accordingly.
3. **Increased Physical Activity:**Doing more physical activity than usual without eating enough or adjusting diabetes medication can rapidly deplete glucose levels.
4. **Alcohol Consumption:** Drinking alcohol, especially on an empty stomach, can interfere with the liver’s ability to release glucose into the bloodstream, leading to decreased blood sugar levels.
5. **Hormonal Deficiencies:** Some essential hormones, such as cortisol or growth hormone, help regulate blood sugar levels. Deficiencies in these hormones can lead to hypoglycemia, particularly in children.

## Symptoms of Low Blood Sugar

The symptoms of low blood sugar (hypoglycemia) can vary from person to person and may differ in severity. Recognizing these symptoms is crucial for prompt and effective treatment, especially for individuals at risk, such as those with diabetes.

### Common Symptoms

- **Physical Symptoms:**

1. Shakiness or trembling, often noticeable in the hands.
2. Sweating can occur even in excellent conditions.
3. Palpitations or a feeling of a rapid, fluttering heartbeat.

- **Cognitive and Emotional Symptoms:**

1. Confusion or difficulty in thinking clearly and swiftly.
2. Irritability or mood changes that seem out of character.
3. Nervousness or anxiety without an apparent cause.
4. Difficulty speaking, such as slurring words or speaking more slowly than usual.

### Severe Symptoms and Complications

1. Seizures or convulsions.
2. Unconsciousness or passing out.
3. Coma in extreme cases.

## Diagnosing Hypoglycemia

Diagnosing hypoglycemia involves assessing both the symptoms and the blood sugar levels. This process can help determine if the symptoms are due to low blood sugar and guide the appropriate management and treatment. Here’s how hypoglycemia is typically diagnosed:

### Blood Sugar Testing

The most direct method for diagnosing hypoglycemia is through measuring blood glucose levels. This can be done via a simple blood test using a glucose metre. A reading below 70 mg/dL typically confirms hypoglycemia.

### Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)

For people with diabetes or those experiencing frequent unexplained symptoms, continuous glucose monitoring can help track glucose levels over time and identify patterns of hypoglycemia.

## Treating and Managing Low Blood Sugar

Treating and managing low blood sugar, or hypoglycemia involves immediate actions to raise the blood sugar to normal levels and long-term strategies to prevent future episodes. Here’s a comprehensive approach:

### Immediate Treatments

**Consume Quick-Acting Carbohydrates:**

- 15-20 grams of glucose or simple carbohydrates is recommended. This could be in the form of glucose tablets, gel, or candy. Alternatively, fruit juice or regular soda (not diet) can also be effective.
- Recheck blood sugar after 15 minutes. If it remains low, consume another 15 grams of carbohydrates.

**Follow-up with Slower-Digesting Carbs:**

Once blood sugar returns to normal, it’s advisable to have a more substantial snack or meal that includes protein and complex carbohydrates. This helps stabilise blood sugar levels and prevent another drop.

### Long-term Management Strategies

**Meal Planning:**

Regularly spaced meals and snacks can help maintain stable blood sugar levels. To slow glucose absorption, include a mix of carbohydrates, protein, and fats in meals.

**Monitor Blood Sugar Levels:**

- Regular monitoring, especially if you are on medications that increase the risk of hypoglycemia, can help you maintain safe blood sugar levels.
- Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) devices are beneficial for getting real-time feedback and trends.

**Exercise Wisely:**

Exercise can cause blood sugar levels to drop. Eating a carbohydrate-rich snack or adjusting medication doses before exercise can prevent hypoglycemia.

## Low Blood Sugar Levels Chart

A chart of blood sugar levels can help understand what constitutes low, average, and [high glucose readings](/blogs/understanding-hyperglycemia-high-blood-sugar), especially for managing diabetes and preventing hypoglycemia. Here is a basic outline of blood sugar levels that reflects typical targets for most individuals.

However, it’s important to note that personal targets may vary based on individual health conditions and should be determined by a healthcare provider.

### Understanding Blood Sugar Readings

**Fasting Blood Sugar (Measured after an overnight fast):**

- **Average:** 70-99 mg/dL
- **Pre-diabetes:** 100-125 mg/dL
- **Diabetes:** 126 mg/dL or higher

**2 Hours After Eating (Postprandial):**

- **Normal:** Less than 140 mg/dL
- **Pre-diabetes:** 140-199 mg/dL
- **Diabetes:** 200 mg/dL or higher

## When to See a Doctor?

- Knowing when to see a doctor for hypoglycemia is essential for effectively managing your health, especially if you are at risk due to diabetes or other conditions that affect blood sugar levels.
- Seeing a doctor regularly and when any of the above conditions apply is crucial to maintaining safe blood sugar levels and overall health.

Located in Banashankari, we offer comprehensive laboratory tests, radiology, and imaging services. Our state-of-the-art technology and skilled team ensure precise and dependable results.

title: CBC Test Explained: What It Measures and Why It's Essential

seo.title: Complete Blood Count Test: What is it and Why You Need It
seo.description: Discover why a complete blood count test is crucial for monitoring your overall health and detecting potential issues.

markdown:
A Complete Blood Count, or CBC, test is a thorough blood test that reveals a patient’s general health. It can also identify potential issues with the person, such as leukemia or anemia. Doctors typically advise completing this test as part of annual health maintenance plans or routine checkup schedules. This test may also be required for patients displaying symptoms including weakness, exhaustion, or fever. Here, we will discuss all the necessary details you need to know before taking this test. So, keep reading.

## **When Is A CBC Performed?**

If you experience signs like these, a [CBC](\) may be necessary:

- Bruising or bleeding
- Tiredness, dizziness, or weakness
- Fever, nausea, and vomiting
- Inflammation like swelling and irritation
- Joint discomfort
- Heart rate or blood pressure issues

## **What Does A CBC Measure?**

The CBC test can reveal a lot about your overall health to your doctor. It measures:

**White blood cells (WBCs)** – WBCs help your body’s immune system. Excessive WBCs can indicate an infection, an allergic reaction, inflammation, or other diseases. You can be more susceptible to illness if it’s low. A low count could also be brought on by a medicine, a virus, or a bone marrow condition.

**Red blood cells (RBC)** – Your body receives oxygen through RBC. They aid in transporting carbon dioxide as well. A low RBC count describes that you may have anemia or another ailment.

**Hemoglobin (Hb or Hgb)** – This is the blood protein that carries oxygen. Aside from blood glucose or HbA1C, this test measures Hb levels.

**Hematocrit (Hct)**– This test determines the percentage of your [blood](\) comprising RBC. Low percentage values may indicate an iron shortage, while high percentages could indicate dehydration or another condition.

**Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV)** – MCV is a scale that assesses the size of your RBCs. These cells’ sizes can give doctors a wealth of knowledge regarding a patient’s health.

**Platelets** – When a patient is injured, platelets begin to work. Your blood’s unique component creates blood clots to block blood flow.

## **Why Should You Take CBC Test?**

Doctors may recommend this test for a variety of reasons, but it is most commonly used for the following.

**Creating a comprehensive report** – Doctors advise taking this test as part of a comprehensive medical examination. The test results assist doctors in determining the overall health of their patients.

**Diagnosing medical conditions** – If you have a disease, your doctor may advise getting a CBC, particularly if it’s difficult to diagnose from the outside.

**Measuring treatment effects** – Occasionally, it is impossible to tell whether a patient is benefiting from the treatment from the outside. In this scenario, your doctor might ask you to have a CBC Test.

**Assessing patient condition** – Your doctor may advise getting a CBC test to check your blood cell count if you have a specific medical condition that affects your RBCs. It will also assist medical professionals in choosing the best course of action.

## **Get Your Test Done With Cadabam's Diagnostics**

Our CBC test package comes with ESR and includes Haemoglobin, Total WBC count, Differential count, RBC count, PCV, Platelet count, MCV, MCH, MCHC, and Red cell distribution width (RDW). Get in touch with us to get a more detailed view of the test.
